reviews.text:
My divorce was finalized February of 2024 so I've had time to reflect on the very poor counsel I received from my Attorney, Brittany. At the time, I didn't receive proper counsel to fully understand the repercussions that have come from her only being concerned about the amount of money she was making. It was clear my case was the lowest priority on her schedule as she would reach out to me 5 minutes before their office closed and rush through her conversation with me. I paid over $12,000 (talk about taking advantage of people in their time of need) for her to respond to a few emails and call me on occasion. It's really criminal how much they charge, especially when going through a divorce. Our mediation was terrible and she did not provide proper counsel. I agreed to things that I didn't fully understand until later because she didn't take the time to explain what that meant to me or care about my case. Her only advice was, "let's take her to court!" Which really just means, "let's drag this out and spend even more money." I mean, she let my ex-wife take half equity in my 4-year old car resulting in me having to pay her thousands for my car! Who takes equity in a car? But I didn't realize how crazy that was until afterwards. There are multiple other things she did not help/counsel on that I am stuck with. She absolutely did not do her due diligence with me.

She really was bad, had a poor attitude and it was so clear that she really didn't care about my case. Now, even after spending thousands of dollars, I am stuck with repercussions that I shouldn't have been stuck with but because of her "legal counsel", I can't doing anything about it. Very disappointed. Lawyers really are terrible, the type of profession that really likes to take advantage of people in their time of need. It's just so disappointing/frustrating to think about. I can't speak for other attorney's there, but I would absolutely ask for another attorney if you end up getting Brittany. And the irony of coming to a Father's-rights law office and being stuck with a female attorney who knows nothing about being a father.

And just so you know, she/they charged about $400/hour and at best, even if things go great and there isn't a lot of issues, you'll spend well over $10,000 to get a divorce. Tell me how that's right? Tell me how that's not taking advantage of people in their time of need?

It really was a horrible situation while already being in a terrible situation with the divorce. Honestly, I would do everything you can to avoid using a lawyer if you can.
reviews.ownerResponse: Cory. We're sorry you feel this way and that it took you so long to come to this conclusion after the close of your case. There's a lot to unpack here, and some misrepresentations made. All we will say is that you have greatly exaggerated the amount you paid us as well as our hourly rate. We wish you would have reached out to us directly to explore what we might be able to do to help. If you feel so inclined, please do so and we will do our best to help in any way possible.

So I asked some questions pertaining to his honesty and integrity, making sure that I would get adequate competent representation, and he declined to answer those questions... The specific questions I asked Chris Jones, at this firm, that has yet to be answered are:

Who is your first duty to, should I retain you?

Corpus Juris Secundum (C.J.S.) legal encyclopedia, volume 7, section 4, His first duty is to the courts and the public, not to the client, and wherever the duties to his client conflict with those he as an officer of the court in the administration of justice, the former must yield to the latter.

Am I considered a "ward of the court"?

According to Section 2 in said Section 7, We find that clients are “wards of the court:”

Clients are also called "wards of the court" in regard to their relationship with their attorneys.

What is a ward of the court? According to a court case, “wards of court” are infants and persons of unsound mind

Wards of court. Infants and persons of unsound mind placed by the court under the care of a guardian. Davis' Committee v Loney, 290 Ky. 644, 162 S.W. 2d 189, 190. Their rights must be guarded jealously. Montgomery v. Erie R. Co., C.C.A.N.J., 97 F.2d 289, 292. See Guardianship.

Am I now, or will I ever be considered "of unsound mind" at any time, whether I retain you or not?

If they can't answer some simple questions to determine whether or not you will get adequate, competent representation, and get some honest answers to make the decision whether you are going to retain them or not, it is best to run away screaming for the hills... I would be VERY WARY of approaching this fellow, or this firm, with your business...

Great law firm. Ayers law specializes in DUI and also Drugs Charges defense.  In March 2010 I was Charged with a DUI so completely bogus that the Driver License Division hearing declared that there was no probable cause for the arrest, so I kept my license and got the Admin fee back [but not the almost $300 tow & storage cost (arrested Fri. night, couldn't pay the Admin fee and get a vehicle release until DMV opened Mon.)]  But I still had the criminal charge to deal with.  In Utah, if you're arrested for anything to do with Drugs, Alcohol, or Sex, no matter how innocent you are you better hire a lawyer.  The prosecution will do whatever they can, no matter how sleazy,  to get a conviction, and they love people without lawyers.  Sure enough, both urine and blood had come back no alcohol, no drugs,  but the prosecutor had added an Obstruction Of Justice charge, a felony.  Probably to scare me into pleading to the phony misdemeanor DUI.  Although a different attorney handled a couple preliminary appearances, Tyler was there for the Driver License Division hearing and the trial.  He demanded that the prosecutor explain and justify the Obstruction charge, and when he couldn't that charge was dropped.  And the DUI was dismissed because there hadn't been any drugs or alcohol in my system.

He had a flat fee for DUI cases, and given that most of them aren't as cut and dried as mine was, and often involve lab tests, witness statements, etc., his  flat fee of $3K was not a lot.  Because I'm a disabled vet with a limited fixed income he took my case for $1K down and let me pay the remaining $2K with however much I could afford each month, and didn't even charge me any interest.  The final payment will be this Aug. 1, 2016.  Not many lawyers will let anyone take 6 years and 5 months to pay off $2K.

My license was never suspended, the DUI and the even more phony Obstruction charge both went out the window, and I was allowed to pay most of the fee as I could.  I don't know how any lawyer could have helped more, and I know that most just plain wouldn't have.

Divorce is an emotional and financially turbulent time and I do my best to not add to the stress by providing candid legal advice and extending latitude from time to time, but given the significant investment of time, and expense, I ask for the same consideration for my work. If that consideration is not returned, then I am, unfortunately,  forced to withdraw.

Based on my positive experience, I highly recommend Robert Tateoka for trust and estate planning services.
